examples.title: Pseudocode: Printer Spooler
examples.description: A pseudocode example demonstrating a printer spooler using a queue
examples.code:
INITIALIZE printQueue as empty queue

WHILE TRUE
 IF new print job arrives THEN
 ADD job to printQueue
 END IF
 IF printer is available THEN
 REMOVE job from front of printQueue
 SEND job to printer
 END IF
END WHILE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how a printer spooler can use a queue to manage print jobs. It continuously checks for new jobs and adds them to the queue. When the printer is available, it removes the job at the front of the queue and sends it to the printer.

examples.title: C: Printer Spooler
examples.description: A C implementation of a printer spooler using a queue
examples.explanation: This C code implements a printer spooler using a queue. It provides functions to initialize the queue, check if it's empty or full, add print jobs to the queue, and process the next job in the queue.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for enqueue and dequeue operations

examples.title: Python: Breadth-First Search
examples.description: A Python implementation of breadth-first search using a queue
examples.code:
from collections import deque

def bfs(graph, start):
 visited = set()
 queue = deque([start])
 visited.add(start)
 while queue:
 node = queue.popleft()
 print(node, end=' ')
 for neighbor in graph[node]:
 if neighbor not in visited:
 queue.append(neighbor)
 visited.add(neighbor)

# Example graph
graph = {
 'A': ['B', 'C'],
 'B': ['A', 'D', 'E'],
 'C': ['A', 'F'],
 'D': ['B'],
 'E': ['B', 'F'],
 'F': ['C', 'E']
}

bfs(graph, 'A')
examples.explanation: This Python code implements breadth-first search using a queue. It uses a set to keep track of visited nodes and a queue to store nodes to be visited.
examples.timeComplexity: O(V + E) where V is the number of vertices and E is the number of edges
examples.spaceComplexity: O(V) for the visited set and queue
